figures.caption: Figure 1. Deployment-centric setup of CL. (a): Training domains (shown in different colors) arrive sequentially; upon deployment, the model is evaluated on an arbitrary target domain. (b): Standard CL methods are prone to learning spurious, domain-specific cues (e.g., the color) and thus fail to classify data from unseen domains at deployment time. (c): Our methods learn domain-invariant representation (e.g., the shape) and thus generalize to both seen and unseen domains. Hence, our evaluation explicitly tests the out-of-domain generalization to ensure that robust patterns are learned, as in works on domain-invariant learning (Krueger et al., 2021; Rame et al., 2022).
Figure 3. Reduced buffer sizes. Average results for 50% (left) and 25% (right) memory capacity, providing less domain informa-
Figure 4. Improvement from ⋆-CL over Naïve-CL.

Table 5. Extended main results: Comparison of our proposed methods against CDA and CTTA baselines on six benchmark datasets. For these baselines, we allow unsupervised updates on the target domain. We report the mean±standard error target domain performance across three independent runs. Results marked as best, second, . . . . .third.
Table 6. Naïve extensions versus CL baselines. Shown: mean±standard error target domain accuracy across three independent runs. Marked: best, second, . . . . . .third.

Table 7. Our methods against the naïve baselines. Our methods considerably outperform their naïve counterparts. Shown: mean±standard error target domain performance across three independent runs, using macro F1 for WM811K and accuracy for the other datasets. Marked: best, second, . . . . .third.
Table 8. Backwards transfer for the main results. Shown: mean±standard error source domain backwards transfer in percentage points across three trials.

Figure 5. Average per-step runtimes over time. We plot the average per-step runtime for methods COPE, STAR, SARL, ⋆-CL-Fishr and ⋆-CL-CORAL across datasets RotatedMNIST (left), CIFAR10C (middle), and TinyImageNetC (right).

Figure 6. Average per-step runtimes over time. We plot the average per-step runtime for methods COPE, STAR, SARL, ⋆-CL-Fishr and ⋆-CL-CORAL across datasets RotatedMNIST (left), CIFAR10C (middle), and TinyImageNetC (right).
Table 9. Runtimes for the methods. Shown: mean±standard error runtimes in minutes across three trials.

Figure 7. Average per-step runtimes over time (extended datasets). We plot the average per-step runtime for methods COPE, STAR, SARL, ⋆-CL-Fishr and ⋆-CL-CORAL across extended datasets (15 source domains) RotatedMNISTExtended (left), WM811KExtended (middle), and Camelyon17Extended (right).
Table 10. Results for disabling the sequential invariance alignment. Shown: mean±standard error target domain accuracy across three independent runs.

Table 11. Ablating the effect of dynamically recomputing Φs′ . We recompute Φs′ for previous domains at the end of the current domain, using the data stored in the replay buffer. For comparison, we give the results for fixing the anchor representations (“Static Φs′”). We report the mean accuracy (macro F1 for WM811K)±standard error target domain performance across three independent runs.
Table 12. Results for a 50% reduced buffer size. Shown: mean±standard error target domain accuracy across three independent runs. Marked: best, second, . . . . . .third.
Table 13. Results for a 75% reduced buffer size. Shown: mean±standard error target domain accuracy across three independent runs. Marked: best, second, . . . . . .third.
Table 14. Source domain performance; computed as described in Section G.8. Shown: mean±standard error source domain accuracy (macro F1 for WM811K) across three independent runs.
Table 15. Results for different target domains. Shown: mean±standard error target domain accuracy across three independent runs. Marked: best, second, . . . . . .third.
Table 16. Results for a reduced compute budget. Shown: mean±standard error target domain accuracy across three independent runs. Marked: best, second, . . . . . .third.
Figure 8. Hyperparameter sensitivity plots (I). We ablate the effect of varying λ and β parameters for ⋆-CL-VREX (left) and ⋆-CL-Fishr (right).
Figure 9. Hyperparameter sensitivity plots (II). We ablate the effect of varying λ and β parameters for ⋆-CL-CORAL (left) and ⋆-CL-MMD (right).

Figure 10. Feature space visualizations (RotatedMNIST). We visualize the feature space of Finetune (left), COPE (middle), and ⋆-CL-CORAL (right) using UMAP (McInnes et al., 2018). Colored numbers denote cluster centroids for the respective class.

Figure 11. Feature space visualizations (Camelyon17). We visualize the feature space of Finetune (left) and ⋆-CL-CORAL (right) using UMAP (McInnes et al., 2018). Colored numbers denote cluster centroids for the respective class.
